definition: The paying off of debt with a fixed repayment schedule in regular installments over a period of time.
Property owned by a person or company, regarded as having value.
A company's financial debt or obligations that arise during the course of its business operations.
The value of the shares issued by a company.
The total amount of income generated by the sale of goods or services related to the company's primary operations.
An amount of money spent.
Financial assets, such as funds held in deposit accounts and/or funds obtained from special financing sources.
A sum of money paid regularly by a company to its shareholders out of its profits.
Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, used as an indicator of the overall profitability of a business.
A reduction in the value of an asset with the passage of time, due in particular to wear and tear.
The availability of liquid assets to a market or company.
The state of being unable to pay the money owed, by a person or company, on time.
An official inspection of an individual's or organization's accounts, typically by an independent body.
A statement of the assets, liabilities, and capital of a business or other organization at a particular point in time.
The total amount of money being transferred into and out of a business.
The difference between an expected and actual result, such as between a budget and actual expenditure.
A prediction or estimate of future events, especially coming weather or a financial trend.
A book or other collection of financial accounts of a particular type.
A list of goods sent or services provided, with a statement of the sum due for these; a bill.
Money owed by a company to its creditors.
Money owed to a company by its debtors.
The difference between the seller's cost for acquiring products and the selling price.
The income returned on an investment, such as the interest received from holding a security.
A performance measure used to evaluate the efficiency or profitability of an investment.
Relating to government revenue, especially taxes.
The degree to which the current assets of an individual or entity exceed the current liabilities of that individual or entity.
Ongoing business expenses not directly attributed to creating a product or service.
An estimation of something's worth, especially one carried out by a professional appraiser.
The final total of an account, balance sheet, or other financial document.
The accumulation or increase of something over time, especially payments or benefits.
The assignment of any authority to another person to carry out specific activities.
The interaction or cooperation of two or more organizations, substances, or other agents to produce a combined effect greater than the sum of their separate effects.
A typical example or pattern of something; a model.
Authority or power given to someone to do something.
Control of an enterprise in every particular and to the smallest detail.
A position of agreement or alliance.
The ability to think about or plan the future with imagination or wisdom.
A plan of action or policy designed to achieve a major or overall aim.
A standard of measurement.
A quantifiable measure used to evaluate the success of an organization, employee, etc., in meeting objectives for performance.
A thing able to be provided, especially a product of a development process.
A point of congestion in a system that slows down the overall process.
Evaluate or check by comparison with a standard.
A system, process, department, etc. that operates in isolation from others.
The action or process of integrating a new employee into an organization or familiarizing a new customer or client with one's products or services.
The rate at which employees leave a workforce and are replaced.
The ability of a company to keep its employees and stop them from going to work somewhere else.
A defining capability or advantage that distinguishes an enterprise from its competitors.
A person with an interest or concern in something, especially a business.
An act or strategy intended to resolve a difficulty or improve a situation; a fresh approach to something.
Commercial or professional procedures that are accepted or prescribed as being correct or most effective.
The capacity to be changed in size or scale.
The energy or mental capacity required to deal with a situation.
A fundamental change in a business strategy.

Relating to or denoting a method of project management, used especially for software development, that is characterized by the division of tasks into short phases of work and frequent reassessment and adaptation of plans.
Acceptance of and willingness to actively support and participate in something.
A point of contact or interaction, especially between a business and its customers or consumers.
The action or process of inheriting a title, office, property, etc.
A general agreement.
The guidance provided by a mentor, especially an experienced person in a company or educational institution.
A list of items to be discussed at a formal meeting.
The written record of a meeting.
A documented event, task, activity, or action that needs to take place.
A group discussion to produce ideas or solve problems.
An agreement or a settlement of a dispute that is reached by each side making concessions.
A thing that is granted, especially in response to demands; a thing conceded.
An alternative proposal made in response to a previous proposal that is regarded as unacceptable or unsatisfactory.
A situation, typically one involving opposing parties, in which no progress can be made.
The power to influence a person or situation to achieve a particular outcome.
Intervention in a dispute in order to resolve it; arbitration.
The use of an arbitrator to settle a dispute.
The minimum number of members of an assembly or society that must be present at any of its meetings to make the proceedings of that meeting valid.
Further action or investigation.
Briefly make or renew contact with someone.
Discussed outside of the current meeting or public forum.
To postpone consideration of a topic.
Break off (a meeting, legal case, or game) with the intention of resuming it later.
A person or thing that makes an action or process easy or easier.
A person who takes part in a dialogue or conversation.
Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement; the most advantageous alternative course of action a party can take if negotiations fail and an agreement cannot be reached.
An analysis or discussion of an event held soon after it has occurred, especially in order to determine why it was a failure.
Convene or cause to convene again, especially after a pause in proceedings.
A daily short meeting to discuss status.
A place during a meeting to note down important topics that are not relevant to the current discussion so they can be addressed later.
A firm decision to do or not to do something.
A condition or requirement that is specified or demanded as part of an agreement.
A non-binding agreement setting forth the basic terms and conditions under which an investment will be made.
Negotiate the terms and conditions of a transaction.
A final demand or statement of terms, the rejection of which will result in retaliation or a breakdown in relations.
The action of working with someone to produce or create something.
Statistical data relating to the population and particular groups within it.
The point at which a recipient of a marketing message performs a desired action.
The buying process that companies lead customers through when purchasing products.
A person or company that has shown interest in a brand's products or services.
The rate at which customers stop doing business with an entity.
The process of dividing a broad consumer or business market into sub-groups of consumers.
The process of evaluating each market segment's attractiveness and selecting one or more segments to enter.
The process of establishing the image or identity of a brand or product so that consumers perceive it in a certain way.
Business to Business; a form of transaction between businesses.
Business to Consumer; the process of selling products and services directly to consumers.
A piece of content intended to induce a viewer, reader, or listener to perform a specific act.
The interactions that a customer has with a brand's presence across different platforms.
The commercial value that derives from consumer perception of the brand name of a particular product or service.
A methodology that attracts customers by creating valuable content and experiences tailored to them.
A traditional method of marketing seeking to push messaging out to potential customers.
A specialized segment of the market for a particular kind of product or service.
A cross-channel content strategy that organizations use to improve their user experience and drive better relationships.
Search Engine Optimization; the process of improving the quality and quantity of website traffic to a website or a web page from search engines.
An innovation, service, or feature intended to make a company or product attractive to customers.
A presentation of a business idea to potential investors or clients.
An unsolicited visit or phone call made by someone trying to sell goods or services.
Sell a different product or service to an existing customer.
Persuade a customer to buy a more expensive item or upgrade a product or service to make a more profitable sale.
A potential customer or client who has been qualified as fitting certain criteria.
Customer Relationship Management; a technology for managing all your company's relationships and interactions with customers and potential customers.
A fixed share of something that a person or group is entitled to receive or is bound to contribute.
A visual representation of sales prospects and where they are in the purchasing process.
The systematic computational analysis of data or statistics.
An organized course of action to promote and sell a product or service.
The percentage of users who take a desired action.
